Kinematics.skill: Unit 1, Topic 1.1 Kinematics - Scalars and Vectors in One Dimension
Kinematics.prompt: This topic covers scalars and vectors in one dimension for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students should understand the fundamental distinction between scalar quantities (magnitude only: distance, speed, time, mass, energy) and vector quantities (magnitude and direction: displacement, velocity, acceleration, force). In one dimension, direction is indicated by positive or negative signs relative to a chosen coordinate system. Students must add and subtract vectors along a line, understanding that displacement is a change in position (delta-x = x_final - x_initial) and can be positive, negative, or zero even when distance traveled is nonzero. Connect to kinematics equations, Newton's laws, and momentum. AP exam note: correctly using signs for direction in one-dimensional problems is critical for success. Vocabulary: scalar, vector, displacement, distance, velocity, speed, magnitude, direction, coordinate system.

Kinematics.skill: Unit 1, Topic 1.2 Kinematics - Displacement, Velocity, and Acceleration
Kinematics.prompt: This topic covers displacement, velocity, and acceleration for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Students should understand that velocity is the rate of change of displacement (v = delta-x / delta-t) and acceleration is the rate of change of velocity (a = delta-v / delta-t). Average velocity considers total displacement over total time, while instantaneous velocity is the velocity at a specific moment (slope of position-time graph). The kinematic equations for constant acceleration are: v = v0 + at, x = x0 + v0*t + (1/2)at^2, v^2 = v0^2 + 2a(delta-x), and x = x0 + (1/2)(v + v0)t. Students must solve problems using these equations, interpret motion graphs, and distinguish between average and instantaneous quantities. Connect to free fall, projectile motion, and Newton's second law. AP exam note: graphical analysis of motion is heavily tested. Vocabulary: displacement, velocity, acceleration, kinematic equations, instantaneous, average, constant acceleration.

This topic covers representing motion for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Students should understand how to interpret and create position-time (x-t), velocity-time (v-t), and acceleration-time (a-t) graphs. The slope of x-t gives velocity; the slope of v-t gives acceleration. The area under a v-t graph gives displacement; the area under an a-t graph gives change in velocity. Motion diagrams (dot diagrams) show an object's position at equal time intervals, with dot spacing indicating speed changes. Students must translate between graphical, mathematical, and verbal representations of motion and recognize constant velocity (linear x-t) vs. constant acceleration (parabolic x-t, linear v-t). Connect to kinematic equations, data analysis, and experimental design. AP exam note: translating between motion representations is a core skill. Vocabulary: position-time graph, velocity-time graph, acceleration-time graph, slope, area under curve, motion diagram.

Kinematics.skill: Unit 1, Topic 1.4 Kinematics - Reference Frames and Relative Motion
Kinematics.prompt: This topic covers reference frames and relative motion for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Students should understand that motion is described relative to a chosen reference frame, and different observers may describe the same motion differently. The velocity of object A relative to object B is found by vector subtraction: v_AB = v_A - v_B (in one dimension). In the reference frame of one moving object, another moving object has a different velocity than in the ground frame. All inertial reference frames (non-accelerating) are equally valid for applying Newton's laws. Students must solve relative velocity problems and explain why different observers may disagree about velocities but agree about accelerations in inertial frames. Connect to Galilean relativity, projectile motion, and forces. AP exam note: relative velocity problems test conceptual understanding of frames. Vocabulary: reference frame, relative velocity, inertial frame, observer, ground frame, Galilean relativity.

Kinematics.skill: Unit 1, Topic 1.5 Kinematics - Vectors and Motion in Two Dimensions
Kinematics.prompt: This topic covers vectors and motion in two dimensions for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 1, Topic 1.5). Students should understand that 2D motion can be analyzed by treating horizontal and vertical components independently. Vectors are resolved into components using trigonometry: Ax = A*cos(theta), Ay = A*sin(theta). Vectors are added by adding components: Rx = Ax + Bx, Ry = Ay + By. The magnitude of the resultant is R = sqrt(Rx^2 + Ry^2) and direction theta = arctan(Ry/Rx). For projectile motion, horizontal velocity is constant (no horizontal acceleration) while vertical motion has constant acceleration g downward. The kinematic equations apply independently to each direction. Students must solve projectile motion problems and add vectors using components. Connect to forces at angles, circular motion, and momentum in 2D. AP exam note: projectile motion and vector component analysis are heavily tested. Vocabulary: component, resultant, vector addition, projectile motion, trajectory, range, horizontal, vertical.

Force and Translational Dynamics.skill: Unit 2, Topic 2.1 Force and Translational Dynamics - Systems and Center of Mass
Force and Translational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers systems and center of mass for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.1). Students should understand that a system is a collection of objects analyzed together, and the center of mass (COM) is the average position of mass in a system, weighted by each object's mass: x_cm = (m1*x1 + m2*x2 + ...) / (m1 + m2 + ...). The center of mass moves as if all external forces act on a single particle at that point with the total system mass. Internal forces between objects within the system do not affect COM motion. For isolated systems, the velocity of the center of mass remains constant (conservation of momentum). Students must calculate COM position and velocity for multi-object systems. Connect to momentum, collisions, and Newton's laws applied to systems. AP exam note: center of mass questions test system-level thinking. Vocabulary: system, center of mass, internal force, external force, weighted average, system boundary.

Force and Translational Dynamics.skill: Unit 2, Topic 2.2 Force and Translational Dynamics - Forces and Free-Body Diagrams
Force and Translational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers forces and free-body diagrams for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Students should understand that forces are vector quantities representing interactions between objects. Common forces include gravitational force (weight, Fg = mg), normal force (perpendicular to surface), tension, friction (static and kinetic), spring force, and applied forces. A free-body diagram (FBD) shows all forces acting on a single object as arrows originating from the object's center, with lengths proportional to magnitude. Only forces ON the object are included, not forces BY the object. Students must draw accurate FBDs for objects in various scenarios (inclined planes, connected objects, suspended objects). Connect to Newton's laws, equilibrium, and acceleration. AP exam note: FBDs are required in nearly every dynamics problem and are explicitly scored. Vocabulary: free-body diagram, normal force, tension, friction, weight, applied force, contact force, field force.

Force and Translational Dynamics.skill: Unit 2, Topic 2.3 Force and Translational Dynamics - Newton’s Third Law
Force and Translational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers Newton’s Third Law for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students should understand that when object A exerts a force on object B, object B simultaneously exerts an equal and opposite force on object A. These action-reaction (third law) pairs are always equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, the same type of force, and act on different objects. They never cancel because they act on different objects. Students must identify third law pairs, distinguish them from balanced forces on the same object, and explain why third law pairs do not prevent acceleration (because they act on different objects). Connect to free-body diagrams, systems analysis, and momentum conservation. AP exam note: distinguishing third law pairs from balanced forces is a common conceptual question. Vocabulary: action-reaction pair, Newton’s third law, interaction, equal and opposite, different objects, force pair.

Force and Translational Dynamics.skill: Unit 2, Topic 2.4 Force and Translational Dynamics - Newton’s First Law
Force and Translational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers Newton’s First Law for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students should understand that an object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity, unless acted upon by a net external force. This law defines inertia: the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. Mass is the quantitative measure of inertia. When the net force on an object is zero, the object is in translational equilibrium (either at rest or moving at constant velocity, meaning acceleration is zero). Students must apply this law to identify equilibrium conditions and recognize that constant velocity requires zero net force. Connect to free-body diagrams, Newton’s second law, and reference frames. AP exam note: conceptual questions often test whether students recognize that motion at constant velocity requires no net force. Vocabulary: inertia, net force, equilibrium, constant velocity, mass, Newton’s first law, translational equilibrium.

Force and Translational Dynamics.skill: Unit 2, Topic 2.5 Force and Translational Dynamics - Newton’s Second Law
Force and Translational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers Newton’s Second Law for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.5). Students should understand that the net force on an object equals its mass times acceleration: F_net = ma (vector equation). The acceleration is in the same direction as the net force. For multi-force problems, resolve forces into components along perpendicular axes, apply F_net = ma independently along each axis. On inclined planes, choose axes parallel and perpendicular to the surface. For connected objects (Atwood machines, objects on surfaces with pulleys), the system can be treated as a whole or each object separately. Students must set up and solve equations of motion for various configurations. Connect to kinematics, work-energy theorem, and circular motion. AP exam note: Newton’s second law problems are the most common quantitative problems on the exam. Vocabulary: net force, mass, acceleration, Newton’s second law, component, free-body diagram, system, Atwood machine.

Force and Translational Dynamics.skill: Unit 2, Topic 2.6 Force and Translational Dynamics - Gravitational Force
Force and Translational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers gravitational force for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Students should understand that every object with mass exerts a gravitational attraction on every other mass. Near Earth's surface, gravitational force (weight) is Fg = mg, where g is approximately 9.8 m/s^2 downward. Newton's law of universal gravitation states F = Gm1m2/r^2 for point masses or spherical objects, where G is the universal gravitational constant. Gravitational field strength g = F/m = GM/r^2 varies with distance from the center of a massive body. On a planetary surface, g depends on the planet's mass and radius. Students must calculate gravitational forces and field strengths and compare them for different scenarios. Connect to orbits, weight vs. mass, and free fall. AP exam note: universal gravitation calculations and conceptual questions about weight are tested. Vocabulary: gravitational force, weight, Newton's law of universal gravitation, gravitational field, g, gravitational constant G.

Force and Translational Dynamics.skill: Unit 2, Topic 2.7 Force and Translational Dynamics - Kinetic and Static Friction
Force and Translational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers kinetic and static friction for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.7). Students should understand that friction is a contact force that opposes relative motion or the tendency of relative motion between surfaces. Static friction (fs) prevents motion and can vary from zero up to a maximum value: fs_max = mu_s * N, where mu_s is the coefficient of static friction and N is the normal force. Kinetic friction (fk) acts during sliding: fk = mu_k * N, and is generally constant and less than maximum static friction. Friction depends on the nature of surfaces and normal force, not on surface area or speed. Students must calculate friction forces and incorporate them into Newton's second law problems. Connect to inclined planes, acceleration, and energy dissipation. AP exam note: friction appears in many dynamics and energy problems. Vocabulary: static friction, kinetic friction, coefficient of friction, normal force, sliding, contact force, mu.

Force and Translational Dynamics.skill: Unit 2, Topic 2.8 Force and Translational Dynamics - Spring Forces
Force and Translational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers spring forces for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.8). Students should understand Hooke's law: the force exerted by a spring is proportional to its displacement from equilibrium and directed toward equilibrium: Fs = -kx, where k is the spring constant (N/m) and x is the displacement from the natural length. The negative sign indicates a restoring force. Stiffer springs have larger k values. Springs store elastic potential energy: PEs = (1/2)kx^2. The spring constant can be determined from the slope of a force vs. displacement graph. Students must calculate spring forces, determine k from data, and apply spring forces in Newton's second law and energy problems. Connect to simple harmonic motion, energy conservation, and oscillations. AP exam note: spring force and energy calculations appear in dynamics and energy units. Vocabulary: Hooke's law, spring constant, restoring force, displacement, elastic, spring force, equilibrium position.

Force and Translational Dynamics.skill: Unit 2, Topic 2.9 Force and Translational Dynamics - Circular Motion
Force and Translational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers circular motion for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 2, Topic 2.9). Students should understand that an object moving in a circle at constant speed has centripetal acceleration directed toward the center: ac = v^2/r = omega^2*r, where v is tangential speed and r is radius. This requires a net centripetal force: Fc = mac = mv^2/r. Centripetal force is not a new type of force but the net inward force provided by gravity, tension, friction, normal force, or combinations. Period T = 2*pi*r/v. Students must identify the force(s) providing centripetal acceleration and apply Newton's second law in the radial direction. Common scenarios include cars on curves, objects on turntables, and vertical circles. Connect to universal gravitation, satellite orbits, and rotational motion. AP exam note: identifying what provides centripetal force is critical. Vocabulary: centripetal acceleration, centripetal force, uniform circular motion, radius, period, tangential speed, radial direction.

Work, Energy, and Power.skill: Unit 3, Topic 3.1 Work, Energy, and Power - Translational Kinetic Energy
Work, Energy, and Power.prompt: This topic covers translational kinetic energy for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that kinetic energy is the energy of motion: KE = (1/2)mv^2, where m is mass and v is speed. Kinetic energy is a scalar quantity and is always non-negative. It depends on the square of velocity, so doubling speed quadruples KE. The change in kinetic energy equals the net work done on the object (work-energy theorem): W_net = delta-KE. Students must calculate kinetic energy, compare kinetic energies of different objects, and apply the work-energy theorem. Connect to work, potential energy, conservation of energy, and momentum. AP exam note: the work-energy theorem is a powerful problem-solving tool that often provides simpler solutions than Newton's laws. Vocabulary: kinetic energy, work-energy theorem, translational, speed, mass, scalar, joule.

Work, Energy, and Power.skill: Unit 3, Topic 3.2 Work, Energy, and Power - Work
Work, Energy, and Power.prompt: This topic covers work for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand that work is the energy transferred to or from an object by a force: W = Fd*cos(theta), where F is the force magnitude, d is the displacement magnitude, and theta is the angle between force and displacement directions. Work is positive when force has a component along the displacement (energy added), negative when opposite (energy removed), and zero when perpendicular (e.g., normal force on a level surface, centripetal force). The area under a force-displacement graph equals work. Net work equals the sum of work done by all forces. Students must calculate work for various force-displacement configurations and interpret F-d graphs. Connect to kinetic energy, potential energy, and power. AP exam note: graphical calculation of work is commonly tested. Vocabulary: work, force, displacement, angle, positive work, negative work, joule, force-displacement graph.

Work, Energy, and Power.skill: Unit 3, Topic 3.3 Work, Energy, and Power - Potential Energy
Work, Energy, and Power.prompt: This topic covers potential energy for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand that potential energy is stored energy associated with the configuration of a system. Gravitational potential energy near Earth's surface: PE_g = mgh, where h is height relative to a chosen reference level. Elastic potential energy stored in a spring: PE_s = (1/2)kx^2. The choice of reference point for gravitational PE is arbitrary but must be consistent. The negative work done by a conservative force (gravity, spring force) equals the change in potential energy: W_conservative = -delta-PE. Potential energy belongs to the system, not individual objects. Students must calculate PE and select appropriate reference levels. Connect to conservation of energy, work, and forces. AP exam note: choosing reference levels and calculating PE changes are important skills. Vocabulary: gravitational potential energy, elastic potential energy, reference level, conservative force, system, stored energy, configuration.

Work, Energy, and Power.skill: Unit 3, Topic 3.4 Work, Energy, and Power - Conservation of Energy
Work, Energy, and Power.prompt: This topic covers conservation of energy for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand that in an isolated system with only conservative forces, total mechanical energy (KE + PE) is conserved: KE_i + PE_i = KE_f + PE_f. When nonconservative forces (friction, air resistance) act, mechanical energy is not conserved: the work done by nonconservative forces equals the change in mechanical energy (W_nc = delta-KE + delta-PE = delta-E_mech). Energy is transformed between kinetic, gravitational potential, elastic potential, and thermal energy, but the total energy of an isolated system remains constant. Students must apply conservation of energy to solve problems involving height changes, springs, and friction. Connect to work-energy theorem, power, and momentum. AP exam note: energy conservation is one of the most powerful and frequently tested problem-solving strategies. Vocabulary: conservation of energy, mechanical energy, conservative force, nonconservative force, isolated system, thermal energy, energy transformation.

Work, Energy, and Power.skill: Unit 3, Topic 3.5 Work, Energy, and Power - Power
Work, Energy, and Power.prompt: This topic covers power for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students should understand that power is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred: P = W/t = delta-E/t. Instantaneous power for a constant force is P = Fv*cos(theta), where v is the velocity and theta is the angle between force and velocity. The SI unit of power is the watt (W = J/s). Higher power means the same work is done in less time, or more work is done in the same time. Students must calculate average and instantaneous power, compare power output of different systems, and relate power to force and velocity. Connect to work, energy, and real-world applications (engines, electrical devices). AP exam note: power calculations linking force and velocity are commonly tested. Vocabulary: power, watt, rate, work, energy transfer, instantaneous power, average power, force, velocity.

Linear Momentum.skill: Unit 4, Topic 4.1 Linear Momentum - Linear Momentum
Linear Momentum.prompt: This topic covers linear momentum for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 4, Topic 4.1). Students should understand that linear momentum is a vector quantity defined as p = mv, where m is mass and v is velocity. Momentum has the same direction as velocity. The SI unit is kg*m/s. Newton's second law can be expressed as F_net = dp/dt (rate of change of momentum). For a system of objects, total momentum is the vector sum of individual momenta. Students must calculate momentum for single objects and systems, and relate momentum to force using Newton's second law. Connect to impulse, conservation of momentum, and collisions. AP exam note: momentum is a fundamental concept tested in collision and explosion problems. Vocabulary: linear momentum, mass, velocity, vector, system momentum, Newton's second law, rate of change.

Linear Momentum.skill: Unit 4, Topic 4.2 Linear Momentum - Change in Momentum and Impulse
Linear Momentum.prompt: This topic covers change in momentum and impulse for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 4, Topic 4.2). Students should understand that impulse is the change in momentum of an object: J = delta-p = F_avg * delta-t. The impulse-momentum theorem states that the net impulse equals the change in momentum. For a constant force, impulse equals force times time interval. For a variable force, impulse is the area under the force-time graph. Increasing the time of interaction (e.g., airbags, crumple zones, catching a ball) reduces the average force for the same impulse. Students must calculate impulse from force-time data and graphs, and apply the impulse-momentum theorem. Connect to collisions, safety design, and Newton's laws. AP exam note: force-time graph interpretation is commonly tested. Vocabulary: impulse, change in momentum, impulse-momentum theorem, force-time graph, average force, contact time.

Linear Momentum.skill: Unit 4, Topic 4.3 Linear Momentum - Conservation of Linear Momentum
Linear Momentum.prompt: This topic covers conservation of linear momentum for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students should understand that when no net external force acts on a system, total momentum is conserved: p_initial = p_final. This applies to collisions, explosions, and any interaction where external forces are negligible. For two-object collisions: m1*v1i + m2*v2i = m1*v1f + m2*v2f. In explosions, initial momentum (often zero) equals total final momentum. Conservation of momentum holds in all directions independently. Students must identify when momentum conservation applies, set up and solve conservation equations, and analyze systems in one and two dimensions. Connect to collisions, center of mass, and Newton's third law. AP exam note: momentum conservation is a primary problem-solving tool for collision problems. Vocabulary: conservation of momentum, isolated system, external force, internal force, collision, explosion, system momentum.

Linear Momentum.skill: Unit 4, Topic 4.4 Linear Momentum - Elastic and Inelastic Collisions
Linear Momentum.prompt: This topic covers elastic and inelastic collisions for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Students should understand that momentum is conserved in all collisions (when external forces are negligible). In elastic collisions, kinetic energy is also conserved (objects bounce apart). In perfectly inelastic collisions, the objects stick together and KE loss is maximum. In general inelastic collisions, some KE is lost to thermal energy, sound, or deformation. To determine collision type, compare KE before and after. For perfectly inelastic: m1v1i + m2v2i = (m1+m2)v_f. For elastic collisions in 1D, use both momentum and KE conservation equations. Students must classify collisions and solve for unknown velocities and energy changes. Connect to energy conservation, momentum, and real-world impacts. AP exam note: distinguishing elastic from inelastic collisions and calculating energy loss are tested. Vocabulary: elastic collision, inelastic collision, perfectly inelastic, kinetic energy conservation, momentum conservation, energy loss.

Torque and Rotational Dynamics.skill: Unit 5, Topic 5.1 Torque and Rotational Dynamics - Rotational Kinematics
Torque and Rotational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers rotational kinematics for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 5, Topic 5.1). Students should understand the rotational analogs of linear kinematic quantities: angular displacement (theta, in radians), angular velocity (omega = delta-theta / delta-t, in rad/s), and angular acceleration (alpha = delta-omega / delta-t, in rad/s^2). The rotational kinematic equations for constant angular acceleration mirror the linear equations: omega = omega_0 + alpha*t, theta = omega_0*t + (1/2)*alpha*t^2, omega^2 = omega_0^2 + 2*alpha*theta. One complete revolution = 2*pi radians = 360 degrees. Students must solve rotational kinematics problems and interpret angular motion graphs. Connect to connecting linear and rotational motion, torque, and rotational dynamics. AP exam note: rotational kinematics parallels translational kinematics. Vocabulary: angular displacement, angular velocity, angular acceleration, radian, revolution, rotational kinematics.

Torque and Rotational Dynamics.skill: Unit 5, Topic 5.2 Torque and Rotational Dynamics - Connecting Linear and Rotational Motion
Torque and Rotational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers connecting linear and rotational motion for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 5, Topic 5.2). Students should understand the relationships between linear and rotational quantities for a point on a rotating object: arc length s = r*theta, tangential velocity v = r*omega, tangential acceleration a_t = r*alpha, and centripetal acceleration a_c = omega^2*r = v^2/r. All points on a rigid rotating body share the same angular velocity and angular acceleration, but points at different radii have different linear velocities and accelerations. The period of rotation T = 2*pi/omega. Students must convert between linear and angular quantities and apply these relationships to pulleys, wheels, and rolling objects. Connect to rotational kinematics, torque, and rolling motion. AP exam note: converting between linear and angular quantities is essential. Vocabulary: tangential velocity, tangential acceleration, centripetal acceleration, radius, arc length, angular velocity, linear-rotational connection.

Torque and Rotational Dynamics.skill: Unit 5, Topic 5.3 Torque and Rotational Dynamics - Torque
Torque and Rotational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers torque for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 5, Topic 5.3). Students should understand that torque is the rotational equivalent of force: tau = rF*sin(theta), where r is the distance from the axis of rotation to the point of force application, F is the force magnitude, and theta is the angle between r and F vectors. Alternatively, tau = r_perpendicular * F = r * F_perpendicular (lever arm method). Torque is a vector: positive for counterclockwise rotation, negative for clockwise (by convention). The unit is Newton-meter (N*m). The net torque determines the angular acceleration of the object. Students must calculate torque for various force-position configurations and determine net torque about a chosen axis. Connect to rotational equilibrium, Newton's second law for rotation, and levers. AP exam note: torque calculations using lever arms are frequently tested. Vocabulary: torque, lever arm, axis of rotation, perpendicular component, Newton-meter, net torque, moment.

Torque and Rotational Dynamics.skill: Unit 5, Topic 5.4 Torque and Rotational Dynamics - Rotational Inertia
Torque and Rotational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers rotational inertia for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 5, Topic 5.4). Students should understand that rotational inertia (moment of inertia, I) is the rotational analog of mass, measuring resistance to angular acceleration. For a point mass: I = mr^2. For extended objects, I depends on the mass distribution relative to the axis of rotation: mass farther from the axis contributes more. Students should know I for common shapes (provided on formula sheet): solid cylinder I = (1/2)MR^2, solid sphere I = (2/5)MR^2, thin hoop I = MR^2, thin rod about center I = (1/12)ML^2. The parallel axis theorem allows calculation about different axes. Students must compare rotational inertias and explain how mass distribution affects rotational behavior. Connect to torque, rotational energy, and angular momentum. AP exam note: comparing moments of inertia for different mass distributions is tested. Vocabulary: rotational inertia, moment of inertia, mass distribution, axis of rotation, point mass, parallel axis theorem.

Torque and Rotational Dynamics.skill: Unit 5, Topic 5.5 Torque and Rotational Dynamics - Rotational Equilibrium and Newton’s First Law in Rotational Form
Torque and Rotational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers rotational equilibrium and Newton’s First Law in rotational form for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 5, Topic 5.5). Students should understand that an object is in rotational equilibrium when the net torque about any axis is zero: sum of tau = 0. In this state, the object either is not rotating or is rotating at constant angular velocity (alpha = 0). For complete static equilibrium, both translational equilibrium (sum of F = 0) and rotational equilibrium (sum of tau = 0) must hold. Students must apply both conditions simultaneously to solve problems involving beams, bridges, and other rigid structures, choosing a convenient pivot point to simplify calculations. Connect to torque, center of mass, and structural analysis. AP exam note: static equilibrium problems requiring both force and torque balance are classic free-response questions. Vocabulary: rotational equilibrium, static equilibrium, net torque, pivot point, angular velocity, translational equilibrium, balanced.

Torque and Rotational Dynamics.skill: Unit 5, Topic 5.6 Torque and Rotational Dynamics - Newton’s Second Law in Rotational Form
Torque and Rotational Dynamics.prompt: This topic covers Newton’s Second Law in rotational form for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 5, Topic 5.6). Students should understand that the net torque on an object equals the product of its rotational inertia and angular acceleration: tau_net = I*alpha. This is the rotational analog of F_net = ma. For objects experiencing both translational and rotational motion (e.g., a pulley with mass, or a ball rolling down a ramp), both F_net = ma and tau_net = I*alpha must be applied simultaneously, often with a constraint relating linear and angular acceleration (a = r*alpha for rolling without slipping). Students must solve problems combining translational and rotational dynamics, such as Atwood machines with massive pulleys. Connect to rotational kinematics, energy, and angular momentum. AP exam note: problems combining F = ma and tau = I*alpha are challenging and heavily tested. Vocabulary: net torque, rotational inertia, angular acceleration, Newton’s second law rotational, rolling constraint, combined dynamics.

Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.skill: Unit 6, Topic 6.1 Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems - Rotational Kinetic Energy
Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.prompt: This topic covers rotational kinetic energy for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand that a rotating object has rotational kinetic energy: KE_rot = (1/2)*I*omega^2, where I is the moment of inertia about the rotation axis and omega is the angular velocity. For an object that is both translating and rotating (like a rolling ball), total kinetic energy = (1/2)*mv^2 + (1/2)*I*omega^2. This total KE must be included in energy conservation problems. Students must calculate rotational KE, compare it for objects with different shapes rolling at the same speed, and apply energy conservation including rotational KE. Connect to conservation of energy, moment of inertia, and rolling motion. AP exam note: energy conservation problems involving rolling objects are a distinctive AP Physics 1 challenge. Vocabulary: rotational kinetic energy, moment of inertia, angular velocity, translational kinetic energy, total kinetic energy, rolling.

Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.skill: Unit 6, Topic 6.2 Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems - Torque and Work
Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.prompt: This topic covers torque and work in rotational systems for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that work done by a torque is W = tau * theta (for constant torque), where theta is the angular displacement in radians. This is analogous to W = Fd in translational motion. The work-energy theorem applies to rotation: net work equals the change in rotational kinetic energy (W_net = delta-KE_rot = (1/2)*I*omega_f^2 - (1/2)*I*omega_i^2). Rotational power is P = tau * omega. Students must calculate work done by torques, apply the rotational work-energy theorem, and compute rotational power. Connect to translational work, energy conservation, and angular kinematics. AP exam note: rotational work and power calculations extend translational concepts. Vocabulary: rotational work, torque, angular displacement, rotational work-energy theorem, rotational power, radian.

Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.skill: Unit 6, Topic 6.3 Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems - Angular Momentum and Angular Impulse
Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.prompt: This topic covers angular momentum and angular impulse for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.3). Students should understand that angular momentum is the rotational analog of linear momentum: L = I*omega for a rotating rigid body, or L = mvr*sin(theta) for a particle moving in a path. Angular impulse equals the change in angular momentum: tau_net * delta-t = delta-L. Newton's second law for rotation can be written as tau_net = dL/dt. The direction of angular momentum is along the axis of rotation (determined by the right-hand rule). Students must calculate angular momentum for rotating objects and particles, and apply the angular impulse-momentum theorem. Connect to conservation of angular momentum, torque, and linear momentum analogy. AP exam note: angular momentum calculations are core to the rotational dynamics unit. Vocabulary: angular momentum, angular impulse, moment of inertia, angular velocity, torque, right-hand rule.

Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.skill: Unit 6, Topic 6.4 Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems - Conservation of Angular Momentum
Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.prompt: This topic covers conservation of angular momentum for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Students should understand that when the net external torque on a system is zero, the total angular momentum is conserved: L_initial = L_final, or I_i * omega_i = I_f * omega_f. Classic examples include an ice skater pulling arms in (I decreases, omega increases), collapsing gas clouds forming stars, and objects landing on rotating platforms. If moment of inertia changes, angular velocity changes inversely to conserve L. Note that kinetic energy may change even when angular momentum is conserved (unlike linear momentum conservation). Students must apply angular momentum conservation and analyze whether KE changes. Connect to rotational dynamics, torque, and system analysis. AP exam note: angular momentum conservation problems are heavily tested. Vocabulary: conservation of angular momentum, net external torque, moment of inertia change, angular velocity change, isolated system.

Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.skill: Unit 6, Topic 6.5 Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems - Rolling
Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.prompt: This topic covers rolling motion for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Students should understand that rolling without slipping requires the constraint v_cm = R*omega, where v_cm is the velocity of the center of mass and R is the radius. Static friction provides the torque for rolling but does no work (no sliding). Total kinetic energy of a rolling object: KE_total = (1/2)*mv^2 + (1/2)*I*omega^2 = (1/2)*(m + I/R^2)*v^2. Objects with different moments of inertia (solid sphere, cylinder, hoop) roll down an incline at different rates: those with less rotational inertia (relative to mass) accelerate faster because more energy goes to translation. Students must apply energy conservation and Newton's second law with rolling constraints. Connect to rotational kinematics, energy, and friction. AP exam note: comparing rolling objects on inclines is a classic problem. Vocabulary: rolling without slipping, static friction, center of mass velocity, rolling constraint, translational, rotational.

Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.skill: Unit 6, Topic 6.6 Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems - Motion of Orbiting Satellites
Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems.prompt: This topic covers motion of orbiting satellites for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Students should understand that a satellite in circular orbit has gravitational force providing centripetal force: GMm/r^2 = mv^2/r, giving orbital speed v = sqrt(GM/r). Orbital period T = 2*pi*r/v = 2*pi*sqrt(r^3/(GM)). Key insights: orbital speed decreases with increasing orbital radius; closer satellites orbit faster. Total energy of an orbiting satellite: E = KE + PE = (1/2)mv^2 - GMm/r = -GMm/(2r). For a stable orbit, total energy is negative. Escape velocity v_esc = sqrt(2GM/r). Students must calculate orbital quantities and analyze energy in orbits. Connect to universal gravitation, circular motion, and conservation of energy. AP exam note: satellite orbit problems combine multiple physics concepts. Vocabulary: orbital velocity, orbital period, gravitational force, centripetal force, escape velocity, orbital radius, Kepler's laws.

Oscillations.skill: Unit 7, Topic 7.1 Oscillations - Defining Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)
Oscillations.prompt: This topic covers defining simple harmonic motion for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.1). Students should understand that simple harmonic motion (SHM) occurs when a restoring force is proportional to displacement from equilibrium: F = -kx (Hooke's law for springs). This produces sinusoidal oscillation about the equilibrium position. Key characteristics: the motion is periodic, the restoring force always points toward equilibrium, acceleration is maximum at maximum displacement (amplitude) and zero at equilibrium, velocity is maximum at equilibrium and zero at amplitude. Examples include mass-spring systems and simple pendulums (for small angles). Students must identify conditions for SHM and describe the qualitative behavior of oscillating systems. Connect to energy, frequency, and wave motion. AP exam note: conceptual understanding of SHM characteristics is foundational. Vocabulary: simple harmonic motion, restoring force, equilibrium, amplitude, oscillation, periodic motion, displacement.

Oscillations.skill: Unit 7, Topic 7.2 Oscillations - Frequency and Period of SHM
Oscillations.prompt: This topic covers frequency and period of SHM for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.2). Students should understand that period T is the time for one complete oscillation and frequency f is the number of oscillations per second: f = 1/T. For a mass-spring system: T = 2*pi*sqrt(m/k), depending on mass and spring constant but NOT amplitude. For a simple pendulum: T = 2*pi*sqrt(L/g), depending on length and gravitational acceleration but NOT mass or amplitude (for small angles). Angular frequency omega = 2*pi*f = 2*pi/T. Students must calculate period, frequency, and spring constants from oscillation data, and predict how changes in parameters affect period. Connect to SHM definition, energy, and wave properties. AP exam note: period calculations and parameter dependence are commonly tested. Vocabulary: period, frequency, angular frequency, mass-spring system, pendulum, hertz, spring constant.

Oscillations.skill: Unit 7, Topic 7.3 Oscillations - Representing and Analyzing SHM
Oscillations.prompt: This topic covers representing and analyzing SHM for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.3). Students should understand that SHM can be represented by sinusoidal graphs of position, velocity, and acceleration vs. time. Position: x(t) = A*cos(omega*t + phi). Velocity leads position by a quarter period (90 degrees). Acceleration is opposite to position (180 degrees out of phase). When position is at maximum, velocity is zero and acceleration is maximum (toward equilibrium). When position is zero (equilibrium), velocity is maximum and acceleration is zero. Students must sketch, interpret, and translate between x-t, v-t, and a-t graphs for SHM, and identify phase relationships. Connect to restoring force, energy, and wave motion. AP exam note: graph interpretation and relating variables at different points in the cycle are heavily tested. Vocabulary: sinusoidal, amplitude, phase, position-time graph, velocity-time graph, acceleration-time graph, quarter period.

Oscillations.skill: Unit 7, Topic 7.4 Oscillations - Energy of Simple Harmonic Oscillators
Oscillations.prompt: This topic covers energy of simple harmonic oscillators for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 7, Topic 7.4). Students should understand that in SHM, energy continuously converts between kinetic energy (KE = (1/2)mv^2) and potential energy (PE = (1/2)kx^2 for springs). Total mechanical energy E = KE + PE = (1/2)kA^2 is constant (conserved, assuming no friction). At maximum displacement (x = A), all energy is potential and KE = 0. At equilibrium (x = 0), all energy is kinetic and PE = 0. At any point: (1/2)mv^2 + (1/2)kx^2 = (1/2)kA^2. The total energy depends on amplitude squared but not on mass or frequency. Students must calculate energies at different positions and interpret energy diagrams. Connect to conservation of energy, amplitude, and damping. AP exam note: energy analysis of oscillating systems is commonly tested. Vocabulary: kinetic energy, potential energy, total mechanical energy, amplitude, energy conservation, energy diagram, energy transformation.

Fluids.skill: Unit 8, Topic 8.1 Fluids - Internal Structure and Density
Fluids.prompt: This topic covers internal structure and density for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 8, Topic 8.1). Students should understand that density (rho) is the mass per unit volume: rho = m/V. The SI unit is kg/m^3. Density determines whether an object floats or sinks: objects less dense than the surrounding fluid float. For fluids, density is generally uniform throughout (for incompressible fluids). Specific gravity is the ratio of a substance's density to water's density (1000 kg/m^3). Students must calculate density, predict floating and sinking behavior, and relate density to molecular structure and phase. Temperature generally decreases liquid density (except water near 4 degrees C). Connect to buoyancy, pressure, and fluid dynamics. AP exam note: density calculations and floating/sinking predictions are foundational for fluid problems. Vocabulary: density, mass, volume, specific gravity, float, sink, incompressible, fluid.

This topic covers pressure for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 8, Topic 8.2). Students should understand that pressure is force per unit area: P = F/A, with SI unit pascal (Pa = N/m^2). In a fluid at rest, pressure at a depth h below the surface is P = P_0 + rho*g*h (gauge pressure = rho*g*h), where P_0 is atmospheric pressure. Pressure acts equally in all directions at a given point in a fluid (Pascal's principle). Atmospheric pressure at sea level is approximately 101,325 Pa (1 atm). Pascal's principle states that pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished throughout: this is the basis of hydraulic systems (F1/A1 = F2/A2). Students must calculate pressure at various depths and apply Pascal's principle. Connect to buoyancy, fluid dynamics, and barometers. AP exam note: hydrostatic pressure and Pascal's principle calculations are commonly tested. Vocabulary: pressure, pascal, gauge pressure, atmospheric pressure, Pascal's principle, hydrostatic, hydraulic.

Fluids.skill: Unit 8, Topic 8.3 Fluids - Fluids and Newton’s Laws
Fluids.prompt: This topic covers fluids and Newton’s laws for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 8, Topic 8.3). Students should understand that buoyant force is the upward force exerted by a fluid on a submerged or partially submerged object. Archimedes’ principle: the buoyant force equals the weight of fluid displaced: F_b = rho_fluid * V_displaced * g. An object floats when its average density is less than the fluid’s density. At equilibrium for a floating object: F_b = F_g (buoyant force equals weight). For a fully submerged object, the apparent weight = actual weight - buoyant force. Students must calculate buoyant forces, determine whether objects float or sink, and find the fraction submerged. Connect to density, pressure, and Newton’s laws. AP exam note: Archimedes’ principle and floating fraction calculations are tested. Vocabulary: buoyant force, Archimedes’ principle, displaced fluid, floating, submerged, apparent weight, density comparison.

Fluids.skill: Unit 8, Topic 8.4 Fluids - Fluids and Conservation Laws
Fluids.prompt: This topic covers fluids and conservation laws for AP Physics 1 students (CED: Unit 8, Topic 8.4). Students should understand two key conservation principles for ideal fluid flow. The continuity equation (conservation of mass): A1*v1 = A2*v2, where A is cross-sectional area and v is flow speed. Where the pipe narrows, fluid speeds up. Bernoulli's equation (conservation of energy per unit volume): P + (1/2)*rho*v^2 + rho*g*h = constant along a streamline. Where fluid speeds up, pressure decreases (and vice versa). Applications include airplane lift, venturi tubes, and pipe flow problems. Assumptions for ideal flow: incompressible, nonviscous, laminar, irrotational. Students must apply both equations to analyze fluid flow systems. Connect to pressure, energy conservation, and real-world applications. AP exam note: applying continuity and Bernoulli's equations together is tested. Vocabulary: continuity equation, Bernoulli's equation, flow rate, streamline, incompressible, laminar flow, ideal fluid.
